narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 44.9% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for admission. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your Year 1 school decile rank of 1—meaning you were in the bottom 10% of your peers—compared to the typical student's midpoint around the 6th decile. Similarly, your Year 3 decile of 2, also well below the average of about 5.5, stood out as the single most influential factor pushing the model toward predicting failure. Your LSAT score of 31, lower than the dataset average of 36.7, was the next biggest driver in that direction. Coming in third was your Year 1 performance, reinforcing the concern about early academic struggles. Your middle family income bracket, around the middle of the distribution, had a moderate influence toward the same prediction, followed by your White racial background, which is common in the dataset at 83.8% but still nudged the odds slightly. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.0, just below the average 3.21, your female gender (represented at 43.6% of students), and your plan for full-time work (typical at 92.7%) rounded out the less dominant but still contributing factors—all tilting the model toward expecting a bar exam challenge. Overall, your profile shows stronger alignment with students who historically struggled on the bar compared to those who passed easily. The good news is that several of these key areas are within your control to improve. Retaking the LSAT to aim above 36 could significantly shift future predictions, as it's a top influencer. Strengthening your academic deciles through better performance in advanced courses or graduate prep would address the biggest red flags from your school rankings. While family income and demographics aren't changeable, focusing on boosting your GPA and building a track record of consistent improvement could help demonstrate growth. We encourage you to seek tutoring, bar prep resources, or even community college courses to build momentum—many students turn predictions around with targeted effort. You're capable of defying this outlook; keep pushing forward.
